titleStmt.title: Learning from Samples: Inverse Problems over measures via Sharpened Fenchel-Young Losses

analytic.title: Learning from Samples: Inverse Problems over measures via Sharpened Fenchel-Young Losses

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Estimating parameters from samples of an optimal probability distribution is essential in applications ranging from socio-economic modeling to biological system analysis. In these settings, the probability distribution arises as the solution to an optimization problem that captures either static interactions among agents or the dynamic evolution of a system over time. Our approach relies on minimizing a new class of loss functions, called sharpened Fenchel-Young losses, which measure the sub-optimality gap of the optimization problem over the space of probability measures. We study the stability of this estimation method when only a finite sample from the optimal distribution is available. The parameters to be estimated typically correspond to a cost function in static problems and to a potential function in dynamic problems. To analyze stability, we introduce a general methodology that leverages the strong convexity of the loss function together with the sample complexity of the forward optimization problem. Our analysis emphasizes two relevant specific settings in the context of optimal transport, where our method provides explicit stability guarantees. The first is inverse unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT) with entropic regularization, where the parameters to estimate are cost functions that govern transport computations; this method has applications such as link prediction in machine learning. The second is inverse gradient flow (iJKO), where the objective is to recover a potential function that drives the evolution of a probability distribution via the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) time-discretization scheme; this is particularly relevant for understanding cell population dynamics in single-cell genomics. Finally, we design and analyze optimization algorithms specifically tailored to efficiently solve iUOT 1 and iJKO problems, and we validate our approach through numerical experiments on Gaussian distributions, where closed-form solutions are available, to demonstrate the practical performance of our methods.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Understanding how probability distributions are coupled is a central challenge across many domains, with applications ranging from socio-economic modeling to the study of dynamic biological systems. Inverse problems in this context seek to recover underlying parameters-such as ground cost functions or potential landscapes-that generate the observed distributions through the resolution of a convex optimization problem. This paper introduces a general methodology based on sharpened Fenchel-Young losses for tackling such inverse problems over the space of probability measures. We focus on two key instances rooted in optimal transport theory: in inverse unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT), the observed distribution corresponds to an optimal coupling, while in inverse Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (iJKO) flow, it represents the optimal time evolution of a measure. For both cases, we provide a detailed analysis of how the number of observed samples affects the accuracy and stability of parameter recovery.

div.head: Previous works
div.p: Inverse optimization and Fenchel-Young losses Inverse optimization [62,1] formalizes the problem of recovering unknown parameters from (possibly noisy) observations of the solution to a parametric optimization problem. Although this is generally a non-convex problem, it becomes tractable when the forward optimization depends linearly on the parameters. In such cases, one can derive a convex formulation for the inverse problem using the Fenchel-Young (F-Y) inequality. This perspective is closely related to the Fenchel-Young loss framework introduced in [8], which we revisit in Section 2.1. For a thorough treatment of the theoretical properties and applications of F-Y losses in machine learning, we refer the reader to [8], and to [13,5] for further theoretical developments. Of particular relevance to our work is the infinite-dimensional setting in which the optimization is carried out over the space of probability measures. This extension has been studied in the context of inverse optimal transport (iOT) in [15,24]. To address more general inverse problems over the space of measures, we propose a new class of loss functions, which we call sharpened Fenchel-Young losses. This generalization encompasses both the generalized F-Y losses from [7] and the Fitzpatrick losses introduced in [4]. A detailed discussion of these losses is provided in Section 2.1.

div.head: Entropic optimal transport
div.p: We focus in this work on two specific settings where the forward optimization problem is derived from optimal transport (OT) with entropic regularization. OT offers a powerful framework for studying the coupling of probability measures, computing a coupling between two measures given a prescribed ground metric, see [50]. In practical applications, OT is combined with entropic regularization, an approach rooted in Schrödinger's work in statistical physics [39]. The pioneering use of entropic regularization for matching was introduced by Galichon in a series of works [30,23,24], the use of entropy is often motivated from the need to account for uncertainty and noise in matchings. Moreover, in the economics literature, the gravity model [25] proposed for studying human mobility and trade flows [3] has be shown to be equivalent to entropic optimal transport [61]. In machine learning literature, entropic regularized OT gained prominence due to its efficient and highly parallelizable computation of OT plans via the Sinkhorn algorithm [56]. The seminal paper by Cuturi [20] introduced the advantages of the Sinkhorn algorithm to the machine learning community, sparking a surge of interest and research activity in this area.

Inverse optimal transport (iOT) Inverse optimal transport (iOT) focuses on understanding observed matchings by assuming they arise as solutions to an optimal transport (OT) problem with an unknown ground cost or metric. The goal in iOT is to recover this underlying metric from observed couplings. Applications include socio-economic modeling, such as analyzing marriage market [23,31], studying human mobility flows-e.g., learning parameters or cost structures in gravity models [55,12]-and assessing the impact of trade flows [32]. As shown in [24], the iOT problem can be formulated as a maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) task, which leads to a convex optimization problem. This convex MLE formulation remains the most widely adopted framework for iOT due to its theoretical guarantees and computational efficiency [15,2]. Our sharpened Fenchel-Young loss framework subsumes this convex MLE approach as a special case and extends naturally to more general inverse problems, such as inverse unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT) and inverse Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto flow (iJKO), which we describe in the following paragraphs. Conditions ensuring uniqueness of solutions in iOT have been characterized in works such as [40,43,23], while [58] provides a Bayesian perspective on the problem. To address potential ill-posedness, several regularization strategies have been proposed, including sparsity-promoting techniques [24,15]. Stability results have been established both in discrete state spaces [16,15] and in continuous settings with sparsity constraints [2]. A range of algorithms have been developed for solving the convex iOT problem, including (proximal) gradient descent [24], block coordinate descent methods that alternate Sinkhorn iterations with gradient steps [43,15], and quasi-Newton methods [2]. In addition, neural network parameterizations for the cost function have been explored [43,42], broadening the scope of algorithmic approaches for iOT.

Forward and Inverse Unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT) The first setting we consider to instanciate our methodology is inverse unbalanced OT (UOT), which generalizes the iOT problem. UOT allow for measures of unequal masses, which is essential in applications where normalization is either not meaningful or where input data is noisy. Early contributions to address this challenge include [35,34,28,47]. A comprehensive framework for unbalanced optimal transport (UOT) was introduced in the seminal work [41], which studied the coupling of positive measures with different masses. For further developments, we refer to [17] for the study of geodesics in UOT, and [37,18] for dynamic formulations. UOT has found diverse applications, including modeling the dynamics of cells [51], machine learning [29], and image processing [44]. In machine learning, UOT has recently garnered attention, leading to contributions such the use of entropic regularization [52], sample complexity results for approximating transport plans from empirical data [52] and the development of efficient Sinkhorn-like algorithms tailored for UOT [53].

Forward and Inverse JKO flows (iJKO) A central research challenge in single-cell analysis is to reconstruct the temporal evolution of cell populations from snapshot observations-that is, empirical distributions of cell states collected at a finite number of time points. One of the first approaches to apply optimal transport (OT) tools to this task is Waddington-OT (WOT) [51], which computes entropic OT plans between consecutive snapshots and "glues" them together to infer likely cell trajectories. A related theoretical perspective is presented in [38], where the evolution is modeled as a drift-diffusion process. To account for processes such as cell proliferation and death-naturally modeled within the framework of unbalanced OT-several extensions have been proposed. These include the use of unbalanced entropic OT [19] and models incorporating branched Brownian motion to reflect cell division and stochastic movement [60]. A complementary but distinct class of approaches relies on diffusion bridges [21,54] to interpolate between snapshots. Although more computationally intensive, these methods have proven valuable when cells can be explicitly tracked across time points [57,46,45], which we do not consider in this work. A more recent line of research, which we build upon in this work, models the evolution between snapshots as the gradient flow of a potential energy functional with respect to the OT distance. In discrete time, this corresponds to the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) scheme. The inverse problem of interest-referred to as iJKO-is to recover the potential functional driving this evolution, given observed distributions at multiple time points. An early contribution in this direction is JKO-Net [11], which uses a bilevel optimization approach to learn a drift functional, representing the potential as a convex neural network. More recently, [59] proposed a simplified convex formulation of JKO-Net, based on minimizing a quadratic objective derived from the first-order optimality conditions of the JKO scheme, to infer the potential from two snapshots. In contrast, we propose to address the iJKO problem using our sharpened Fenchel-Young loss framework. This formulation allows for the inclusion of entropic regularization in the functional, enabling the modeling of diffusion phenomena and facilitating a refined theoretical analysis of the method's stability with respect to sampling noise, which we detail in this work.

div.head: Our contributions
div.p: • In Section 2, we present sharpened Fenchel-Young losses for solving inverse problems over measures. These losses are a generalization of Fenchel-Young and Fitzpatrick losses, which are essentially derived from generalizations of the Fenchel-Young inequality. A key advantage of sharpened F-Y losses is that they a convex differentiable framework for studying inverse problems over measures. The section ends with an abstract stability result that we explore for special settings in the subsequent sections.

• In Section 3, we present the problem of inverse unbalanced optimal transport. In Section 3.1, we present a new sample complexity result for the forward problem of unbalanced optimal transport. The proof is based on ideas from convex analysis (inspired by the proof for balanced OT in [49]) and hence does not require the smoothness assumptions in the previous result [53]. We then study the FY loss in the context of iUOT and establish local strong convexity and local Lipschitz smoothness properties which then lead to O(1/ √ n) error bounds on the empirical estimator under n-iid samples.

• In Section 4, we present the problem of inverse gradient flows, and focus on the problem of learning a potential function from two observations of the JKO flow. We show that the recovery of a potential functions from two measures links by a JKO step (iJKO) can be studied under the framework of iUOT, and thereby inherit the sample complexity bounds derived in Section 3.5.

• Section 5 presents model consistency results for the sharpened FY losses with specific sample complexity results in the context of iUOT under nondegenearcy conditions. Section 6 then numerically checks these nondegeneracy conditions and verifies that they lead to model consistency in practice.

Some of these contributions build upon and significantly extend our earlier conference publication [2], which analyze the impact of sampling noise on the stability of the inverse optimal transport (iOT) problem.

div.head: Notation and definitions
div.p: The set of continuous functions over a X is denoted by C(X ), and the space of square integrable functions (with respect to α) is denoted by L 2 (α); L 2 0 (α) denotes the set of f ∈ L 2 (α) that satisfy f dα = 0. A function f defined on a space with norm ∥•∥ is said to be γ-Lipschitz if ∥f (x)-f (y)∥ ⩽ γ|x-y| ; it is said to be γ-Lipschitz smooth if its gradient is γ-Lipschitz. To keep the notation light, integrals are replaced by duality pairings, i.e., f dα is abbreviated by ⟨f, α⟩; the notation ⟨•, •⟩ is also used for the inner product in a Hilbert space, but no confusion should arise. Expectation is denoted by E, i.e., E x∼α [f ] denotes ⟨f, α⟩. For any function φ, its Fenchel conjugate is denoted by φ * and is defined via φ * (y) = sup x ⟨x, y⟩ -φ(x). The space of probability measures over X is denoted by P(X ) and the space of positive measures by M + (X ); the probability. If α is a probability measure on N i=1 X i , then α i denotes its ith marginal; if it is a finite positive measure the m(α) denotes its mass. The φ-divergence associated to a convex, positive, lower-semi-continuous function φ : (0, ∞) → [0, ∞) that satisfies φ(1) = 0 is defined as
div.formula: D φ (α|β) := X φ dα dβ (x) dβ(x) + φ ′ ∞ X dα ⊥ (x),
div.p: where φ ′ ∞ = lim x→∞ φ(x)/x, and where, for any pair (α, β) ∈ M + (X ), α ⊥ is defined via the Radon-Nikodym-Lebesgue decomposition α = (dα/dβ)β + α ⊥ (see [52]). The Kullback-Leibler divergence is abbreviated by KL and corresponds to D φ with φ(p) = p log(p) -p + 1. The indication function of a set S is denoted by ι S and the notation ι =β abbreviates the D {1} -divergence, i.e., it is defined by ι =β (α) = 0 if α = β and +∞ otherwise. For any two symmetric matrices A and B, the notation A ⪰ B means that A -B is positive semi-definite, and λ max (A), λ min (A) denote, respectively, the largest and smallest eigenvalue of A. The direct sum notation for functions, i.e., f ⊕ g is defined by (f ⊕ g)(x, y) = f (x) + g(y) and the tensor notation for measures is defined by (α⊗β)(A, B) = α(A)β(B), where A and B are measurable sets. The Big-O notation O(g) means that there exists a constant such that . . . , e.g. f = O(g) means that there exists a constant c such that f ⩽ cg. The closed ball of center x and radius r with respect to the metric d(•, •) is denoted by Bd (x, r). The dual of a norm ∥ • ∥ is denoted by ∥ • ∥ * .
We will use the notation f c = g to mean that f, g are equal up to additive constants, i.e. f (x) = g(x) + C for constant C.

div.head: Preliminaries on the gap function
div.p: The gap function is central to the analysis and development of many optimization algorithms [10,6], and will be central to the development of loss functions in this work. Definition 1. Let U be a Banach space and U * be the dual space. Given Ω :
div.formula: U → R, the gap function is ∀u ∈ U, v ∈ U * , G Ω (v; u) := Ω(u) + Ω * (v) -⟨v, u⟩(1)
div.p: where Ω * , the convex conjugate of Ω, is defined over v ∈ U * .
It is well known that
div.formula: 1. v → G Ω (v; u) is convex in v, regardless of whether Ω is convex.
div.p: 2. By the Fenchel-Young inequality, G Ω (v; u) ⩾ 0 for all v and u and the Fenchel-Young equality states that G Ω (v; P (v)) = 0 where P (v) := argmax u ⟨v, u⟩ -Ω(u).

div.head: Inverse problems over measures
div.p: Let Z be a metric space. Suppose that a measure µ ∈ M + (Z) and a function f ∈ C(Z) are related via the following optimization problem
div.formula: µ = P Ω (f ) := argmin µ∈M + (Z) f (z)dµ(z) + Ω(µ),(2)
div.p: where Ω : Z → R is some functional such that for all f ∈ C(Z), µ is uniquely defined.
The noiseless inverse problem Given the measure P Ω (f ), recover the function f .

The noisy inverse problem Given some 'noisy' observation μ ∈ M + (Z) of P Ω (f ), recover the function f . The following framework can be applied for any observation in M + (Z), but the setting that is most relevant to the applications considered in this work is where μ = m n n i=1 δ z i where z i is sampled uniformly from the normalized version of P Ω (f ) and m is an estimate of the mass of P Ω (f ). For this inverse problem, we are also faced with the difficulty we have access only to a noisy version Ω of Ω, rather than Ω itself.

Example 1 (Inverse optimal transport). Let Z = X ×Y and for some α ∈ P(X ) and β ∈ P(Y), define Ω(µ) = KL(µ|α ⊗ β) + ι µ 1 =α + ι µ 2 =β , where µ 1 , µ 2 are the first and second marginals of µ. In this case, given f ∈ C(X × Y) representing some cost function, µ = P Ω (f ) is the entropic optimal transport plan between α and β. The noiseless inverse problem corresponds to recovering the cost f from this optimal transport coupling µ. The noisy inverse problem is to recover f given (x i , y i ) iid ∼ µ for i = 1, . . . , n. In this case, the noisy observation is the empirical measure μ = 1 n n i=1 δ (x i ,y i ) and we have access only to the functional
div.formula: Ω(µ) = KL(µ|α ⊗ β) + ι µ 1 = α + ι µ 2 = β where α = 1 n n i=1 δ x i and β = 1 n n i=1 δ y i .
div.p: Example 2 (Inverse gradient flow). Let Z = X and let µ 0 ∈ P(X ). Given τ > 0, Ω µ 0 (µ) =
div.formula: 1 τ W 2 2,ε (µ, µ 0 ) where W 2,ε is the entropic Wasserstein functional W 2 2,ε (µ, µ 0 ) = inf π 1 =µ,π 2 =µ 0 ∥x -x ′ ∥ dπ(x, x ′ ) + εKL(π|µ ⊗ µ 0 ).
div.p: Then, (2) corresponds to one-step of a JKO scheme
div.formula: µ 1 = argmin µ∈P(X ) ⟨f, µ⟩ + 1 τ W 2 2,ε (µ, µ 0 ).
div.p: The inverse problem seeks to recover f from µ 1 . The practical noisy setting is that we have empirical samples x i iid ∼ µ and y i iid ∼ µ 0 for i = 1, . . . , n. So, in this case, μ = 1 n n i=1 δ x i and we have access to the functional
div.formula: Ω(µ) = 1 τ W 2 2,ε (µ, μ0 ), where μ0 = 1 n n i=1 δ y i .

div.head: The sharpened Fenchel-Young loss
div.p: Our inverse problem requires recovering a function f from a measure μ which, in the noiseless setting, are related via the optimization problem (2). Naively, one might consider min f ∥μ -µ(f )∥ 2 for some appropriate norm ∥ • ∥, but this typically leads to a nonlinear and nonconvex optimizaton problem. Instead, in this work, we advocate tackling this problem using the Sharpened Fenchel-Young loss, which is defined with respect to some discrepancy D and the functional Ω from (2).

Definition 2 (The Sharpened Fenchel-Young loss). Let Ω : Z → R and D : M + (Z) × M + (Z) → [0, ∞] be such that D(µ|µ) = 0 for all u ∈ U. Given μ ∈ M(Z), the sharpened Fenchel-Young loss associated to Ω is
div.formula: L(f ; μ, Ω, D) = G Λ μ (-f ; μ)
div.p: where Λ μ(µ ′ ) := Ω(µ ′ ) + D(µ ′ |μ) and G Λ μ is the gap function from (1).
Remark 1. Key insight behind this loss: If μ = P Ω (f ), then
div.formula: μ = P Λ μ (f ) = argmax µ ⟨f, µ⟩ -Ω(µ) -D(µ|μ).(3)
div.p: So, for fixed μ, our loss is the gap function associated to the functional µ → Ω(µ) + D(µ|μ).
Proposition 1 (Key properties). For all s ⩾ 0, the sharpened F-Y loss satisfies the following properties:
div.formula: 1. f → L(f ; μ, Ω, D) is convex.
div.p: 2. Non-negativity: L(f ; μ, Ω, D) ⩾ 0 for all f ∈ C(Z) and μ ∈ M(Z).
3. If μ = P Ω (f ), then L(f ; μ, Ω, D) = 0. The reverse implication also holds if Λ μ is strictly convex.
4. If Λ μ is strictly convex, then L(f ; μ, Ω, D) is differentiable, with
div.formula: ∇L(f ; μ, Ω, D) = μ -P Λ μ (f ), where P Λ μ (f ) = argmin µ∈M(Z) {⟨f, µ⟩ + Λ μ(µ)}, 5. Given two discrepancies 0 ⩽ D ′ ⩽ D, L(f ; μ, Ω, D ′ ) ⩾ L(f ; μ, Ω, D).
div.p: Proof. These properties are obvious, so we provide only some brief comments here:
• Convexity and non-negativity are inherited from properties of the gap function.
• For L(f ; μ, Ω, D) = 0 if μ = P Ω (f ), note that since D(µ|µ) = 0, we have P Ω (f ) = P Λ μ (f ), so equality follows from the Fenchel-Young equality for the gap function.
• Differentiability is due to the envelope theorem.
• The final property is due to non-negativity of D.
We mention two special cases below.

Fenchel-Young losses In the case where D ≡ 0, L 0 (f ; μ) := L(f ; μ, Ω, 0) is the so-called Fenchel-Young loss, which was first formalized in [8]. In this case, L 0 (f ; μ) = G Ω (-f ; μ) = Ω(μ) + Ω * (-f ) + ⟨f, μ⟩ is the gap-function associated to Ω. The main difficulty in computing the loss is related to the nonlinear part Ω * (-f ).

To sharpen or not to sharpen? From the viewpoint of minimizing over f , if D ≡ 0, then the data μ appears only in the linear term ⟨f, μ⟩, while taking non-trivial discrepancies D means that the data also influences the nonlinear part Λ * μ(f ). Any curvature of the loss comes from this nonlinear part, our abstract stability theorem (Theorem 1) shows that one can guarantee stable recovery provided that the loss is locally Lipschitz and strongly convex (i.e. locally bounded by quadratics) and the forward map is stable. For certain applications, such as inverse optimal transport as we present in Section 3.3, it is sufficient (and easiest) to let D ≡ 0 and this is already widely used in the literature [24]: in this case, Ω * (-f ) is already locally strong convex and strongly convex and its computation amounts to solving a forward entropic OT problem. However, in other cases, such as inverse gradient flows as presented in Section 4.2, inserting the data into the nonlinear part using an appropriate D introduces these local curvature properties and can also lead to easier computations.

Fitzpatrick losses Recently, Fitzpatrick losses [48] were introduced, based on the Fitzpatrick function, which extends the gap function to monotone operators. Fitzpatrick losses are a special case of sharpened Fenchel-Young losses where D is taken to be the Bregman divergence defined by Ω, i.e. D(u|u
The relationship between Fitzpatrick losses and Fenchel-Young losses was investigated in [48,14], and one particular relationship shown in [14] is that the FY loss upper bounds the Fitzpatrick loss:
div.formula: G Ω (f ; μ) ⩾ G Ω+D Ω (•|μ) (f ; μ).
div.p: Note that this property is a special case of property 5 listed above. Note that adding any discrepancy always decreases the function value, hence the name 'sharpened' Fenchel Young loss.

Example 3 (Quadratic forms on vector spaces). Even though this is not the purpose of the sharpened FY losses, we provide a simple example using quadratic functions on vector spaces R n to illustrate the sharpening effect of our loss. Let x, y ∈ R n and given Ω : R n → R, D : R n × R n → R are defined by Ω(y) = 1 2 ⟨Ay, y⟩ for some symmetric positive definite matrix A ∈ R n×n and D(y ′ , y) = r 2 ∥y ′ -y∥ 2 for r > 0. Then,
div.formula: P (x) = argmin y∈R n ⟨x, y⟩ + 1 2 ⟨Ay, y⟩ = -A -1 x.
div.p: We have Λ y ′ (y) = 1 2 ⟨Ay, y⟩ + r 2 ∥y -y ′ ∥ 2 . The sharpened FY loss is
div.formula: L(x; ŷ, Ω, D) = G Λ ŷ (-x; ŷ) = ⟨x, ŷ⟩ + 1 2 ⟨Aŷ, ŷ⟩ + max y∈R n ⟨-x, y⟩ - 1 2 ⟨Ay, y⟩ - r 2 ∥y -ŷ∥ 2 = ⟨x -rŷ, ŷ⟩ + 1 2 ⟨Aŷ, ŷ⟩ + r 2 ∥ŷ∥ 2 + 1 2 ⟨(A + rId) -1 (x -rŷ), x -rŷ⟩ = 1 2 (A + rId) -1 2 (x -rŷ) + (A + rId) 1 2 ŷ 2
div.p: Taking r > 0 improves the conditioning of the loss.

Linear parameterization From Proposition 1, in the noiseless setting where µ = P Ω (f ) and Ω is strictly convex, we can recover f by minimizing min f ∈C(Z) L(f ; µ, Ω, D). In practice, one would like to solve for the function f under a simplified hypothesis space, and to preserve convexity of our sharpened Fenchel-Young loss, it is sufficient to consider linear parameterizations. Let h 0 , h 1 , . . . , h S be fixed Lipschitz continuous functions on Z, and define for θ ∈ R S ,
div.formula: Ψθ = h 0 + S s=1 θ s h s .
div.p: We then consider functions parameterized by θ, f θ (z) = (Ψθ)(z) and we consider the following finite dimensional convex optimization problem:
div.formula: inf θ∈R S L(Ψθ; µ, Ω, D). (4
div.p: Regularization In the noisy setting where we observe μ ≈ P Ω (f ) (possibly some sampled version), the inverse problem typically becomes ill-posed which necessitates some regularization. Moreover, the functional Ω might be unavailable and we have access only to an approximation Ω. We therefore consider instead inf θ∈R S L(Ψθ; μ, Ω, D) + λR(θ)
div.formula: (P λ (μ, Ω))
div.p: for some λ > 0. To preserve convexity, R : R s → R is typically taken to be a proper, convex, lower semicontinuous function. Moreover, in practice, we would like to enforce some structure on the recovered θ, and two choices of R that will be discussed are ℓ 1 (to enforce sparsity) and nuclear norm (to enforce low-rank).

div.head: Stability results
div.p: In the following abstract stability result, the two assumption relates to stability of the measurements μ and also stability of the forward map P Λ when considered against the test functions h i , our parameterization functions. The third assumption on local Lipschitz-smoothness and local strong convexity of the sharpened Fenchel-Young losses is a local curvature condition on the loss. Under stability of the forward map and local curvature conditions, we have stable recovery of the parameter θ ⋆ .

In general, verification of these conditions can be challenging and must be done on a caseby-case basis. The two main conditions to check are the stability of the forward map and local curvature of the perturbed loss. For eOT, stability of the forward map has been studied in [49] for independent marginals -note that stability of the forward map here in this case would involve samples of the coupling μ and so the marginal data is not entirely independent as in the standard complexity analysis of eOT. So, for the study of iUOT, we present a more general stability result in Theorem 2. In Propositions 2 and 3, we tackle the local curvature conditions: prove that our loss is indeed locally strongly convex and locally Lipschitz smooth. For iJKO, the analysis of recovering potential functions can be reduced to the study of iUOT (see Section 4.3).

Theorem 1. Let µ ⋆ = P Ω (Ψθ ⋆ ) and ε > 0. Suppose we have approximate data μ and an approximate functional Ω such that Local curvature F (θ) := L(Ψθ; μ, Ω, D) is Lipschitz smooth and α-strongly convex on the ball B K (0) for some K > ∥θ ⋆ ∥;
Measurement stability ∥Ψ * (μ -µ ⋆ )∥ ⩽ ε/2 (measurement stability);
div.formula: Forward stability ∥Ψ * P Λ (Ψθ ⋆ ) -P Λ(Ψθ ⋆ ) ∥ ⩽ ε/2, where Λ(µ) = Ω(µ) + D(µ, μ) and Λ(µ) = Ω(µ) + D(µ, µ ⋆ ).
div.p: If the perturbation ε and regularization parameter λ are sufficient small with respect to α and
then, there exists a unique minimizer of (P λ (μ, Ω)), denoted by θ, which satisfies

Proof. The idea is to use iii) of Lemma 2, which, for a locally strongly convex and Lispchitz smooth function, establishes when an approximate first order condition at a point θ ⋆ implies the existence of a minimizer and bounds its proximity to θ ⋆ . To this end, note that ∇ F (θ ⋆ ) = Ψ * (μ -P Λ(Ψθ ⋆ )) and, since µ ⋆ = P Ω (Ψθ ⋆ ), it follows from 3. in Proposition 1 that
where the last inequality follows from the two last assumptions (measurement and forward map stability). Lemma 2 is now enough to conclude the result.
3 Inverse unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT)
In this section, we study the application of F-Y losses for the unbalanced optimal transport problem of recovering a cost function given sampled observations of some coupling measure.

To emphasise that we are covering a cost function from coupled data, the unknown will be denoted using c ∈ C(X × Y) (this corresponds to the function f from the previous section) and the observations are couplings π ∈ M + (X × Y) (corresponding to µ from the previous section).

div.head: The forward problem (UOT)
div.p: As shown in Theorem 1, stability of the associated inverse problem depends on stability of the forward problem. To this end, we begin with a description of the forward problem and present in Theorem 2 a sample complexity result, demonstrating the stability of UOT coupling, given sampled versions of α and β. This is an original contribution as it extends the sample complexity results of [53] to the setting where the divergences φ 1 and φ 2 are not necessarily smooth. Moreover, it allows the marginals to be loosely coupled, a technicality required for studying the inverse problem.
Given measures α ∈ M + (X ), β ∈ M + (Y) and a cost function c ∈ C(X × Y), the unbalanced optimal transport computes a coupling π ∈ M + (X × Y) as
div.formula: π = argmin π∈M + (X ×Y) ⟨c, π⟩ + ηKL(π|α ⊗ β) + D φ 1 (π 1 |α) + D φ 2 (π 2 |β) (p-UOT)
div.p: for some f -divergence D φ and where π 1 , π 2 denote the marginals of π. The balanced optimal transport setting is the special case where Under Assumption 1, there exists a unique π solving p-UOT and its Radon-Nikodym density with respect to α ⊗ β is given by
div.formula: D φ 1 (π 1 |α) = ι π 1 =α and D φ 1 (π 2 |β) = ι π 2 =β
dπ dα ⊗ β = exp f ⋆ ⊕ g ⋆ -c /η , α ⊗ β -a.e.,(5)
div.p: where (f ⋆ , g ⋆ ) are solutions to the dual problem, i.e.,
div.formula: (f ⋆ , g ⋆ ) ∈ argmin (f,g)∈L 2 (α)×L 2 (β) K α,β (f, g), (d-UOT)
div.p: where K α,β is defined as

The solutions (f, g) of (d-UOT) are called Kantorovich potentials and if c is Lipschitz continuous, then they are continuous with the same Lipschitz constant as c. So, the optimization of K α,β can be cast in L 2 (α) × L 2 (β). Proposition 9 summarises formally these known facts.

div.head: Stability of the forward problem
div.p: In the case of balanced OT,
div.formula: K α,β (f, g) = K α,β (f + λ, g -λ)
div.p: for all λ ∈ R and one can show that K α,β can be restricted to optimization on

and K α,β is strongly convex on the P 0 with respect to the norm ∥f ∥ L 2 (α) +∥g∥ L 2 (β) -this observation was a key idea behind the sample complexity analysis of balanced OT in [49]. In general, under the compactness assumption, one can also write inf f,g K α,β (f, g) = inf f,g∈P 0 T α,β (f, g) for some translation invariant operator (see Proposition 10) with T α,β being strongly convex on P 0 . As a result, one always has stability of the forward map (see Proposition 11. The main obstacle is to quantify this stability: depending on the divergences φ 1 and φ 2 , T α,β might not be differentiable and its (sub)gradients generally has a more complex dependency on α, β compared to the balanced OT setting. To obtain sample complexity bounds, we will make the following assumptions on the divergences φ 1 : the first assumption implies that K α,β can be restricted to optimization over
for some constant M while the second assumption ensures that K α,β is strongly convex on bounded sets of the form B M .

Assumption 2. There exists a, a ′ ∈ dom(φ 1 ) and b, b ′ ∈ dom(φ 2 ) such that bm β > am α and b ′ m β < a ′ m α , Assumption 3. Assume that φ * 1 is locally strongly convex Remark 2. The relationship between local strong convexity and local lipschitz smoothness was studied in [33]. In particular, if φ 1 is coercive, proper and lsc, then φ * 1 being locally strongly convex is equivalent to φ 1 being locally Lipschitz smooth. Assumption 2 ensure that K α,β is coercive over f, g. Some examples are 1. Jensen Shannon:
div.formula: φ 2 (t) = t log(t) + (1 + t) log((1 + t)/2). or KL, φ 2 (x) = x log(x). Here, dom(φ 2 ) = R + . Since 1 ∈ dom(φ 1
div.p: ) by definition, Assumption 2 is trivially true since we can take b arbitrarily large.
div.formula: χ 2 : φ 2 (x) = (x -1) 2 or hellinger: φ 2 (t) = ( √ t -1) 2 .
div.p: Assumption 2 also trivially holds just as for the KL setting since dom(φ 2 ) contains arbitrarily large values.
div.formula: 3. α-divergence with α ∈ (0, 1), with φ 2 (t) = 4 (1-α 2 ) (1 -t (1+α)/2
div.p: ) Moreover, all the examples above are locally Lipschitz smooth, so Assumption 3 hold.

The proof to the following result, presented in Appendix D.3, is inspired by the proof of [49] for the balanced optimal transport setting, making use of the PL inequality in convex analysis. Note in the theorem, the observed samples (z i , w i ) are assumed to be drawn from some coupling ξ with marginals 1 mα α and 1 m β β. This encompasses the classical setting where z i and w i are drawn independently from 1 mα α and 1 m β β but also takes into the account the setting where they can be coupled, as in the iUOT setting.

Theorem 2 (Unbalanced eOT's sample complexity). Suppose that assumptions 1 holds. Assume also that either D φ 1 (π 1 |α) = ι π 1 =α and D φ 1 (π 2 |β) = ι π 2 =β (we are in the balanced OT setting) or Assumptions 2 and 3 hold. Let (z i , w i ) n i=1 iid ∼ ξ, where ξ has marginals ξ 1 = α/m α and ξ 2 = β/m β . Let π denote the unbalanced optimal transport coupling with cost c, i.e., the minimizer attaining (p-UOT), and, similarly, let π n denote the optimal transport coupling with cost c but with α and β replaced by their empirical counterparts α n := m α /n δ z i and
div.formula: β n := m β /n δ w i .
div.p: Then, for all t > 0 and test function h ∈ L ∞ (α ⊗ β), with probability at least 1 -exp(-t),
div.formula: ⟨h, π -π n ⟩ ≲ ∥h∥ L ∞ (α⊗β) • m α m β (log(n) + t) n
div.p: Crucially, the implicit constant depends on c only via ∥c∥ ∞ .
In the following we only present the main idea of the proof. For a full proof see Appendix D.3.

Main idea behind the proof. The case of balanced OT was discussed in [49] so we focus on the unbalanced OT setting under Assumptions 2 and 3. Under assumptions 1 and 2, the minimizers (f, g) to K n := K α n ,β n are uniformly bounded by constants dependent on m α , m β , diameters of X and Y and the Lipschitz constant of c, but independent of n. Moreover, assumptions 1 and 3 imply that K n is strongly convex on bounded sets (again, the local strong convexity parameters do not depend on n). Let f ⋆ ∞ , g ⋆ ∞ be the minimizers to the population dual function K α,β and f ⋆ n , g ⋆ n be the minimizers to the empirial dual function K α n ,β n . The difference between π n and π can be written in terms of the error between the Kantorovich potentials, due to the characterization in (5). Thanks to strong convexity,
div.formula: ∥f ⋆ n -f ⋆ ∥ 2 L 2 (α n ) + ∥g ⋆ n -g ⋆ ∥ 2 L 2 (β n ) strong convexity ≲ K n (f ⋆ ∞ , g ⋆ ∞ ) -K n (f ⋆ n , g ⋆ n ) PL inequality ≲ ∥∇K n (f ⋆ ∞ , g ⋆ ∞ )∥ 2 . Finally, ∥∇K n (f ⋆ ∞ , g ⋆ ∞ )∥ 2 can be easily bounded by O(1/ √ n) using concentration inequalities and observing that ∇K α,β (f ⋆ ∞ , g ⋆ ∞ ) = 0.

div.head: The inverse problem (iUOT)
div.p: The inverse unbalanced optimal transport (iUOT) seeks to recover the cost c ⋆ , given samples of the optimal coupling π ⋆ and the reference marginals α ⋆ and β ⋆ , under the assumption that these objects can be related via the optimization problem (p-UOT). We mention two special cases which are of practical interest: Metric Learning using Inverse optimal transport (iOT) Inverse Optimal Transport, as introduced initially in [24] can be seen as a special case of our iUOT problem, because classical (balanced) OT is a special case of uOT. Given observations of some probability coupling π ⋆ ∈ P(X × Y) with marginals α ⋆ , β ⋆ , the goal of iOT is to find a metric c ⋆ (x, y) on X × Y under the assumption that
div.formula: π ⋆ = argmin π∈Π(α ⋆ ,β ⋆ ) ⟨c ⋆ , π⟩ + ηKL(π|α ⋆ ⊗ β ⋆ ),(7)
div.p: where
is the set of probability couplings with marginals α, β. This is precisely the iUOT setting with φ = ι =1 and φ * (y) = y for both
div.formula: φ ∈ {φ 1 , φ 2 }. So, D φ 1 (•|α ⋆ ) = ι =α ⋆ and D φ 2 (•|β ⋆ ) = ι =β ⋆ .
div.p: The observations of π ⋆ are typically assumed to be i.i.d. samples (x i , y i ) ∼ π ⋆ for i = 1, . . . , n. This setting is for instance used for studying matching in various economic application [43,24,15].

Link completion using Inverse Unbalanced (iUOT) A related problem to iOT is link completion, which arises where x i and y i are sampled observations of measures α ⋆ and β ⋆ which are not necessarily of the same mass. Moreover, out of the samples {x i } N i=1 and {y i } M i=1 , only a small number of links (x i k , y i k ) n k=1 with n ≪ max(N, M ) are observed. The goal is to recover an underlying metric and the unobserved links. This is a natural setting that one would model using iUOT with different divergences
div.formula: φ 1 , φ 2 . Let α N = 1 N N i=1 δ x i , β M = 1 M M i=1 δ y i and π n = 1 n n k=1 δ (x i k ,y i k )
div.p: . Using balanced iOT is problematic because π n does not have marginals α N , β N , thus it is not feasible in the inner optimization problem Ω * αN ,β M (-c). To cope with this issue, one can apply iUOT, for instance with KL discrepancies, i.e. apply iUOT with
div.formula: Ω α N ,β M (π) = ηKL(π|α N ⊗ β M ) + τ KL(π 1 |α N ) + τ KL(π 2 |β M ).

div.head: Using the Fenchel-Young loss for iUOT
div.p: In this section, we describe how to apply the Fenchel-Young loss for iUOT, for this part, it is sufficient to let
div.formula: D ≡ 0. For π ∈ M + (X × Y) and c ∈ C(X × Y), α ∈ M + (X ) and β ∈ M + (Y), let Ω α,β (π) = ηKL(π|α ⊗ β) + D φ 1 (π 1 |α) + D φ 2 (π 2 |β),
div.p: ad note that its convex conjugate Ω * α,β (-c) corresponds to the forward UOT problem. The Fenchel-Young loss is
div.formula: L(c; π, Ω α,β , 0) := ⟨c, π⟩ + Ω * α,β (-c) + Ω α,β (π),
div.p: From the viewpoint of minimizing over c, the last term Ω α,β (π) is simply a constant, so this term can be dropped, and as a slight abuse of notation, the Fenchel-Young loss throughout this section will be written as
div.formula: L iUOT (c; π, α, β) = ⟨c, π⟩ + Ω * α,β (-c). (FY-iUOT)
div.p: Kantorovich formulation of the Fenchel-Young loss In general, the computation of the FY loss in (FY-iUOT) requires solving the forward iUOT problem Ω * α,β (-c) which is itself a maximization problem. So, min c L iUOT (c; π, α, β) is a-priori a min-max problem. The following lemma shows that this can be written purely as an unconstrained minimization problem, which then allows for the use of quasi-Newton solvers such as L-BFGS (see Section 6).
Lemma 1 (Kantorovich form of the iUOT FY loss). Let L(c; π, α, β) be as in (FY-iUOT). Then, inf c∈C(X ×Y)
div.formula: L(c; π, α, β) = inf c∈C(X ×Y),f ∈C(X ),g∈C(Y) K π,α,β (f, g, c)
div.p: where
(Kanto-FY-iUOT)
Proof. By the Strong Duality Proposition 9 we can rewrite
div.formula: Ω * α,β (-c) = inf f ∈C(X ),g∈C(Y) ⟨φ * 1 (-f ), α⟩ + ⟨φ * 2 (-g), β⟩ + η⟨exp((f ⊕ g -c)/η), α ⊗ β⟩.
div.p: The empirical loss function and regularization In practice, one observes empirical versions 2 of π ⋆ , α ⋆ , β ⋆ . That is, for i = 1, . . . , n,
div.formula: (x i , y i ) iid ∼ 1 m π ⋆ π ⋆ , z i iid ∼ 1 m α ⋆ α ⋆ and w i iid ∼ 1 m ⋆ β β ⋆ ,
div.p: where m(ν) denotes the mass of the measure ν. That is, we have access to
div.formula: π n = m π ⋆ n δ (x i ,y i ) , α n = m α ⋆ n δ z i and β n = m β ⋆ n
div.p: δ w i . To this end, we consider the regularized FY loss (FY-iUOT) by replacing Ω α ⋆ ,β ⋆ with Ω α n ,β n and π ⋆ by π n , leading to the regularized optimization problem
div.formula: argmin θ L(Ψθ; π n , α n , β n ) + λR(θ), (iUOT n )
div.p: where
div.formula: π n = m π ⋆ n i δ (x i ,y i ) , α n = m α ⋆ n i δ z i and β n = m β ⋆ n i δ w i .

div.head: Local curvature properties of the loss
div.p: Consider the cost parametrization Ψ :
div.formula: θ ∈ R s → h 0 + θ ⊤ h ∈ C(X × Y), where h = (h i ) s i=1 and h i ∈ C(X × Y).
div.p: In this section, we will discuss strong convexity and lipschitz smoothness of the loss
div.formula: F(θ) := L(Ψθ; π, α, β) = inf f,g K π,α,β (f, g, Ψθ)(8)
div.p: 1 NB: We use a similar notation to ( 6), but note that we previously discussed properties of K α,β with fixed cost c, whereas the iUOT loss has a similar formulation that we now denote by K π,α,β . The relationship between the two is that K π,α,β (f, g, c) = K α,β (f, g) + ⟨c, π⟩.

2 In this work, we will assume that the masses m π ⋆ , m α ⋆ , m β ⋆ are known. In practice, these are estimated and although our theory can be extended to cater for inexact masses, for simplicity, we will focus on the setting of known masses so that only uncertainty arises from sampling.
For the sake of notation simplicity, since α, β, π are fixed throughout this section, we omit the dependency on π, α, β and simply write K = K π,α,β .

We first observe that the minimizers of K are unique up to a potential ambiguity of f (x) + g(y). Indeed, the primal solution π to iUOT is unique and there exist f, g, c such that the density dπ/d(α ⊗ β) = exp((f ⊕ g + c)/η). So, if (f, g, c) and (f ′ , g ′ , c ′ ) are solutions, then:

In the case of iOT, the cost is identifiable only up to f (x) + g(y) for some functions f ∈ C(X ) and g ∈ C(Y). For iUOT, this ambiguity is not necessarily a problem, depending on properties of the divergences D ϕ . To ensure identifiability of the cost parameterization, we will make the following assumption on the cost parameterization. Define the marginal costs
div.formula: h 1 (x) := h(x, y)dβ(y), h 2 (y) := h(x, y)dα(x), h 12 := h(x, y)dα(x)dβ(y).
div.p: and the centred costs

Note that h0 is centred with respect to α, β so that h0 (x, y)dα(x) = h0 (x, y)dβ(y) = 0 for all x, y. While h1 be the β-centred with h1 (x, y)dβ(y) = 0 for all x and h2 is α-centred with h2 (x, y)dα(x) = 0 for all y.
Assumption 4. Assume that at least one of the following is true:
div.formula: i) ⟨ h0 ⊗ h0 , α ⊗ β⟩ ∈ R s×s is invertible with smallest eigenvalue ρ min .
div.p: ii) ⟨ h1 ⊗ h1 , α ⊗ β⟩ ∈ R s×s is invertible with smallest eigenvalue ρ min , φ * 2 is locally strongly convex, and Assumption 2 holds.
iii) ⟨h ⊗ h, α ⊗ β⟩ ∈ R s×s is invertible with smallest eigenvalue ρ min , both φ * 1 and φ * 2 are locally strongly convex, and Assumption 2 holds.

Note that (i) in the assumption ensures injectivity of the centred cost functions, to remove the potential ambiguity in perturbations of the form f (x) + g(y). On the other hand, in the case where there is strong convexity in φ * 1 and φ * 2 , the invertibility assumption is on the costs h rather centred versions, since the Kantorovich potentials would be uniquely identifiable.

Proposition 2 (strong convexity). Let assumptions 1 and 4 hold. Let B > 0. Let ∥θ∥ ⩽ B. Then, F defined in (8) is µ-strongly convex on {θ ; ∥θ∥ ⩽ B} with µ = C 1 ρ min exp(-C 2 B/η), where C 1 , C 2 are constants that depend only on h, and on φ 1 and φ 2 in the cases of ii) and iii).
Proof. We first consider the case where i) of Assumption 4 holds. We have the decomposition
div.formula: Ψ = Ψ 0 + Ψ 1 + Ψ 2 where Ψ 0 θ = h 0 + θ ⊤ h0 , Ψ 1 θ = θ ⊤ h 1 , Ψ 2 θ = θ ⊤ (h 2 -h 12 ).
div.p: Assume that ∥θ∥ ⩽ B. Then, Ψ 0 θ, Ψ 1 θ, Ψ 2 θ are all Lipschitz with Lipschitz constant linearly dependent on B.
Note that Ψ 1 θ ∈ C(X ) and Ψ 2 θ ∈ C(Y).
Step 1: decomposing K into convex and strongly convex terms Then, inf

For the last line, following the proof of Proposition 10, optimization over f and g can be restricted to a bounded set, we can restrict the optimization over f, g to P 0 = (f, g) ; f dα = 0, ∥f ⊕ g∥ ∞ ⩽ M for some M > 0 depending linearly on B. By defining
div.formula: G 0 (f, g, θ) := ⟨Ψ 0 θ, π⟩ + η⟨exp f ⊕ g -Ψ 0 θ η , α ⊗ β⟩ G 1 (f, g, λ, θ) := ⟨Ψ 1 θ + Ψ 2 θ, π⟩ + ⟨φ * 1 (-f -λ + Ψ 1 θ), α⟩ + ⟨φ * 2 (-g + λ + Ψ 2 θ), β⟩, we can write inf f,g K(f, g, Ψθ) = inf f,g∈P 0 ,λ∈R G 0 (f, g, θ) + G 1 (f, g, λ, θ)
div.p: Note that G 1 is convex and G 0 is locally strongly convex over ∥θ∥ ⩽ B and (f, g) ∈ P 0 by Lemma 5.
Step 2: strong convexity over G 0 implies strong convexity of F.

Then, F(θ t ) = inf f,g∈P 0 ,λ∈R G 1 (f, g, λ, θ t ) + G 0 (f, g, θ t ) ⩽ G 1 (f t , g t , λ t , θ t ) + G 0 (f t , g t , θ t ) ⩽ tG 0 (f, g, θ) + (1 -t)G 0 (f ′ , g ′ , θ ′ ) + tG 1 (f, g, λ, θ) + (1 -t)G 1 (f ′ , g ′ , λ ′ , θ ′ ) - µt(1 -t) 2 ∥Ψ 0 (θ -θ ′ )∥ 2 L 2 (α⊗β) .

div.p: Local strong convexity and lipschitz smoothness for the empirical loss We can show that when the number of samples n is sufficiently large, the matrices in Assumption 4 evaluated against α n ⊗ β n instead of α ⊗ β will be close (using matrix concentration bounds), so we have the following result, telling us that on bounded sets, F n (θ) = F(Ψθ; α n , β n , π n ) is strongly convex and Lipschitz smooth with constants uniformly over n.
Corollary 1. Assume that assumption 1 and 4 hold. Assume also that
div.formula: m α m β • t + log(s) ρ min ≲ √ n.
div.p: Then, with probability at least 1 -e -t 2 , for ∥θ∥ ⩽ B, F n is strongly convex with constant µ ∼ ρ min e -CB/η strongly convex and Lipschitz smooth with costant K ∼ ρ max e -CB/η , where C is a constant that depends only on h and the divergences φ 1 , φ 2 .
Proof. This is a direct consequence of the previous two propositions and a concentration bound on ∥⟨α n ⊗ β n , h ⊗ h⟩ -⟨α ⊗ β, h ⊗ h⟩∥ given in Proposition 16

Suppose that ((x i , y i ), z i , w i ) n i=1 iid ∼ ξ, where ξ 1 = π ⋆ , ξ 2 = α ⋆ , and ξ 3 = β ⋆ and where π ⋆ , α ⋆ and β ⋆ satisfy (11). 4 Inverse gradient flow (iJKO)

div.head: Learning functional in gradient flows
div.p: Suppose for some curve of probability measures α t ∈ P(X ), t ∈ [0, T ], one observes samples
The goal is to recover some underlying potential function driving the evolution of α t . A typical forward model for this problem is the Jordan-Kinderleher-Otto (JKO) implicit stepping
div.formula: α k+1 = argmin α∈P(X ) J(α) + 1 τ W 2 2 (α, α k ), (12
div.p: where W 2 2 is the (possibly entropy regularized) Wassserstein-2 distance, τ > 0 is some timestep, and J : P(X ) → R is some functional to be learned. One can view {α k } k as the discretization of the curve of probability α(t, •) that solve the PDE
where ∇ and div are spatial gradient and divergence operators. The inverse problem is to recover the functional J given sample observations of α t .
Some typical examples of J include i) linear potentials J(α) := ⟨V, α⟩,
ii) linear potential with diffusion J(α) := ⟨V, α⟩ + rKL(α|α ref ) for some reference measure α ref ,
iii) interactions potentials J(α) := ⟨V, α ⊗ α⟩, e.g., V (x, y) = 1/ ∥x -y∥ ζ for repulsion.
To account for uncertainty in π, it is sometimes interesting to replace the Wasserstein distance W 2 with the entropy regularized Wasserstein distance
div.formula: W 2 2,η (α, β|R) := inf π∈Π(α,β) ∥x -x ′ ∥ 2 dπ(x, x ′ ) + ηKL(π|R),
div.p: where R is some reference measure (typically taken to be α ⊗ β). In this work we focus on the first setting i) of linear potentials with entropic Wasserstein gradient flow.

div.head: iJKO for learning potentials
div.p: We consider the iJKO setting where the functional J(α) to be learned is linear in α. i.e. it is of the form J(α) = ⟨V, α⟩ = V (x)dα(x) and we assume that
div.formula: α k+1 = argmin α∈P(X ) ⟨V, α⟩ + 1 τ W 2 2,η (α, α k |α ⊗ α k ). (JKO)
div.p: Remark 3. The more general JKO setting (e.g. interaction potentials) is more challenging since the forward problem ( 12) can be nonconvex, moreover, the coupling ⟨V, α ⊗ α⟩ would require extending our sharpened FY loss to consider more intricate structured measures (i.e. the tensor α ⊗ α in the interaction case). For these reasons, we leave the more general setting as an interesting venue for future work and focus on the setting of learning linear potentials for now.

The iJKO loss Consider the sharpened Fenchel-Young loss from Definition 2 with D = rKL(•|α k+1 ) being the KL divergence with some scaling parameter r > 0, and Ω being the entropic Wasserstein functional, that is
div.formula: Λ r (α) = W 2 2,η (α, α k |α ⊗ α k ) Ω(α) + rKL(α|α k+1 ) D(α|α k+1 )
div.p: So, the sharpened FY loss for iJKO loss becomes
div.formula: L r iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) := ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ -inf α∈P(X ) ⟨V, α⟩ + 1 τ W 2 2,η (α, α k |α ⊗ α k ) + rKL(α|α k+1 ), (FY-iJKO)
div.p: where we've dropped the constants
div.formula: 1 τ W 2 2,η (α k+1 , α k |α k+1 ⊗ α k ) + rKL(α k |α k+1 ). Remark 4.
div.p: • Adding in the sharpening term rKL(α|α k+1 ) leads to easier computations and analysis since if fixes the support of α in the inner problem to be that of α k+1 . If we set D ≡ 0 and directly considered the Fenchel-Young loss, then, the loss corresponds to
div.formula: ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ + Ω * (V ) = inf f,g ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ + ⟨f, α k ⟩ s.t. -V + g + η τ exp - (f (x ′ ) + g(•) + ∥• -x ′ ∥ 2 /τ ε/τ dα k (x ′ ) ⩾ 0.
div.p: Due to the constraint, this loss is more difficult to compute and also less regular than the loss obtained with sharpening.
• Note that if we let r → 0, then the problem becomes
div.formula: L 0 iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) = ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ -inf α∈P(X ) ⟨V, α⟩ + 1 τ W 2 2,η (α, α k |α k+1 ⊗ α k ) = inf g -⟨g, α k ⟩ + ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ + η τ exp τ η -V (x) + g(x ′ ) - ∥x -x ′ ∥ 2 τ dα k (x ′ )dα k+1 (x).
div.p: In the unconstrained setting min V L 0 iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) corresponds to solving an eOT problem and -V is the Kantorovich potential, note however that one typically enforces some structure on the potential function V , so this correspondence to eOT is not true in general. As r → ∞, we show that this loss converges to a quadratic loss (see Proposition 5).
Proposition 4 (Alternative formulations). Let r ′ = r -η/τ . Then, (i) Kantorovich formulation:
div.formula: L r iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) = inf f,g ⟨V + r ′ exp(- f r ′ ), α k+1 ⟩ -⟨g, α k ⟩ + η τ ⟨exp τ (f ⊕ g -c V ) η , α k+1 ⊗ α k ⟩, (Kanto-iJKO)
div.p: (ii) Link to iUOT Let α k+1 and α k satisfy (JKO). Then, α k+1 and α k are marginals of π k+1 , the solution to the following UOT problem
div.formula: π k+1 = argmin π 2 =α k V (x) + ∥x -x ′ ∥ 2 τ dπ(x, x ′ ) + η τ KL(π|α k+1 ⊗ α k ) + sKL(π 1 |α k+1 ) (13)
div.p: for any s > 0. Moreover, letting c

div.p: for any coupling π that has first marginal π 1 = α k+1 .
Proof. Consider the inner problem in (FY-iJKO),
div.formula: A = inf α∈P(X ) V (x)dα(x) + 1 τ W 2 2,η (α, α k |α ⊗ α k ) + rKL(α|α k+1 ).(14)

div.p: Finally, for the links to iUOT, note that α k+1 is the minimizer to (15), and so, α k+1 and α k are the marginals to (13). Note also that given any π with first marginal being α k+1 ,
div.formula: L r iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) = ⟨V, α k+1 ⟩ + A = L iUOT (c V ; π, α k , α k+1 ) -∥x -x ′ ∥ 2 dπ(x, x ′ )
div.p: and from the point of view of minimizing over V , the term ∥x -x ′ ∥ 2 dπ(x, x ′ ) is a constant.

div.head: Sample complexity of the iJKO problem
div.p: Thanks to the link to iUOT, we can apply the sample complexity result of iUOT. In particular, given sampled measures αk and αk+1 , the sample complexity for iJKO is equivalent to the sample complexity of L iUOT (c V ; π, αk , αk+1 ) for any π with π1 = αk+1 . So, we can in particular apply Theorem 3 under the assumption that π consists of iid samples of π k+1 (the true coupling between α k+1 and α k ) and consider αk+1 = π1 .
In this case, due to the KL discrepancy for π 1 , ϕ * 1 is strongly convex. So, the assumption 4 implies that under the parametrization
div.formula: V (x) = θ ⊤ h(x), letting h(x) = h(x) -h(x)dα k+1 (x), we require that E x∼α k+1 [ h(x) h(x) ⊤ ] is invertible. Observe that from (Kanto-iJKO), K(V + b, f, g -b) = K(V, f, g
div.p: ) for all b ∈ R and one can only expect convergence up to constants -in this sense, this assumption of invertibility of the centred functions is natural.

div.head: Example 4. Let us expand on what it means for M = E x∼α k+1 [ h(x) ⊗ h(x)
div.p: ] to be invertible in the context of Gaussians. Suppose that α k+1 ∈ N (0, Σ k+1 ), α k ∈ N (0, Σ k ) and V (x) = x ⊤ θx for θ ∈ R d×d . Note that we cannot expect to recover skew-symmetric matrices θ, i.e. θ = -θ ⊤ , since ⟨θx, x⟩ = ⟨θ ⊤ x, x⟩ = 0 if θ is skew-symmetric. This is precisely captured in the requirement that M is invertible: in this case, h(x) = xx ⊤ and we then have
div.formula: h(x)dα k+1 (x) = Σ k+1 and h(x) = xx ⊤ -Σ k+1 .
div.p: It follows, by Isserlis's theorem that
div.formula: E x∼α k+1 [ h(x) ⊗ h(x)] = (E x∼α k+1 [x i x j x k x ℓ ]) (i,j),(k,ℓ) -Σ k+1 ⊗ Σ k+1 = Σ k+1 ⊗ Σ k+1 + Σ k+1 ⊗ Σ k+1 T
div.p: where Tvec(B) = vec(B T ). The kernel of E x∼α k+1 [ h(x) h(x) ⊤ ] is the space of skew-symmetric matrices.
Corollary 2. Let x i iid ∼ α k+1 and y i iid ∼ α k for i = 1, . . . , n. Consider the parameterization V θ = θ ⊤ h where h(x) = (h i (x)) s i=1 and assume that
div.formula: M = E x∼α k+1 [ h(x) ⊗ h(x)] ∈ R s×s
div.p: is invertible. Suppose that α k+1 , α k satisfy (JKO) with V (x) = ⟨θ ⋆ , h(x)⟩. Then, with probability at least 1 -e -t , θλ n is unique and
div.formula: θλ n -θ ⋆ = O( (log(n) + t)/n) + O(λ)
div.p: where the implicit constants depend only on η/τ , condition number of M and Lipschitz constants of h.

div.head: Relationship to the quadratic loss
div.p: In the unparameterized setting, if α k and α k+1 are related via (JKO), the potential function is precisely the Kantorovich potential function, that is V = -τ -1 ∇f * (α k+1 , α k ). Based on this insight, a simple quadratic loss function was introduced in [59] for the iJKO problem, the loss was derived using the first order condition with respect to Wasserstein gradient):
div.formula: F (θ) := ∥∇V θ (x k+1 ) + τ -1 (x k+1 -x k )∥ 2 dγ(x k , x k+1 )
div.p: where γ is the transport plan between α k and α k+1 . We can rewrite this in terms of T the transport map as F (θ) = ∥∇V θ (y) + τ -1 (y -T (y))∥ 2 dα k+1 (y). Moreover, since the transport map is related to the Kantorovich potential f * (α k+1 , α k ) via ∇f * (α k+1 , α k ) = y -T (y), it follows that

Minimizing lightspeed loss corresponds to a 2 step process since one first needs to compute the OT plan π, then minimize a quadratic loss. In contrast, our approach directly minimizes a loss given the data α k , α k+1 which leads to easier theoretical analysis. In the next result, we draw a parallel between this quadratic loss and our approach.

In the following proposition, we consider F r (θ) := L r iJKO (V θ ; α k , α k+1 ) from (FY-iJKO) where r is the sharpening parameter: as we increase the parameter r, we are increasingly enforce the compliance to the data α k+1 in the loss leading to a 'sharpening' of our iJKO loss.

In the limit at r = ∞, we also obtain a quadratic loss, which corresponds to an H 1 norm to fit the Kantorovich potential as opposed to an L 2 norm in the lightspeed loss. Similarly to lightspeed loss, the minimization of F r (•) + λ 0 /r∥ • ∥ 1 corresponds to a two step procedure where the first step amounts to an optimal transport problem and the second to Lasso-like problem. Note that from the final property of Proposition 1, F r is monotonically decreasing as r → ∞, and in fact, we have F r (θ) = O(1/r) in the iJKO setting. So, one needs to scale the regularization parameter λ appropriate to obtain a meaningful limit.
Proposition 5 (Sharpening effects of the FY loss). The Fenchel-Young loss associated to α k+1 and α k with r > 0 satisfies
div.formula: lim r→∞ t F r (θ) - 1 τ W 2 2,η (α k+1 , α k |α k+1 ⊗ α k ) = V θ + τ -1 f * (α k+1 , α k ) 2 dα k+1 . (16

div.formula: inf θ V θ + 1 τ f ⋆ (α k+1 , α k ) 2 dα k+1 + λ 0 R(θ),

div.p: The statement about minimizers is an immediate consequence of Gamma convergence.

div.head: Model Consistency via Dual Certificates
div.p: Let us now consider the regularized empirical objective of the form
div.formula: F (θ) = L(Ψθ; μ, Ω, D),
div.p: and define the regularized estimator θλ ∈ arg min
div.formula: θ F (θ) + λR(θ),
div.p: where R is a convex regularizer that promotes structure in θ, such as sparsity (e.g., via the ℓ 1 -norm) or low-rankness (e.g., via the nuclear norm). Theorem 1 showed conditions under which there is primal stability on θλ -θ ⋆ .

In this section, we discuss conditions under which θλ correctly identifies the structure (e.g., sparsity pattern or rank) of a ground truth θ ⋆ . This phenomenon is known as model consistency or manifold identification. It can be studied abstractly via geometric properties of the regularizer R, and in particular via mirror-stratifiable structure [27]. On a high level, mirror stratifiable regularizers exhibit strong regularity properties: their domain can be partitioned into a collection of stratas (manifolds), such that on each strata, R is regular. For example, ℓ 1 -norm ∥x∥ 1 = n i=1 |x i | is nonsmooth but is smooth on any M I := {x ; Supp(x) = I} for I ⊂ {1, . . . , n}. We begin with the general statement which holds for any mirror stratifiable regularizer, a broad class that includes ℓ 1 norm, ℓ 1,2 norm and nuclear norm.
Meta-theorem: Manifold Identification [27, Theorem 1] Let θ ⋆ denote a structured ground truth (e.g., sparse or low-rank). Suppose there exists a dual vector q ⋆ ∈ ∂R(θ ⋆ ) satisfying:
div.formula: • Approximate dual certificate: ∥q -q ⋆ ∥ ⩽ δ, • Estimator proximity: ∥ θλ -θ ⋆ ∥ ⩽ δ,
div.p: • Nondegeneracy: q ⋆ lies in the relative interior of ∂R(θ ⋆ ), denoted as q ⋆ ∈ ri(∂R(θ ⋆ )).
Then, for sufficiently small δ, the estimator identifies the active manifold of the ground truth:
div.formula: M θλ = M θ ⋆ .
div.p: Here, M θ denotes the active manifold at θ, i.e., the strata that θ belongs to and on which R is smooth.

div.head: Examples:
div.p: • For the ℓ 1 -norm, M θ corresponds to all vectors with the same support as θ.
• For the nuclear norm, M θ corresponds to all matrices with the same rank as θ.

div.head: Geometric Interpretation
div.p: The nondegeneracy condition q ⋆ ∈ ri(∂R(θ ⋆ )) guarantees that small perturbations of the dual certificate q remain in the same face of the subdifferential. This ensures stability of the structural identification: if q ⋆ lies on the boundary of the subdifferential, then even infinitesimal changes in the data could shift q outside ∂R(θ ⋆ ), leading to incorrect support recovery. For mirror stratifiable regularizers, due to a one-to-one correspondence between the primal stratas that partition dom (∂R) and the dual stratas that partition dom (∂R * ) = ran (∂R), primal and dual proximities along with nondegeneracy somehow constrain θλ and θ ⋆ to lie on the same strata. Here, dom denotes the domain and ran denotes the range.

div.head: Verifying the conditions for model consistency
div.p: The first order optimality condition implies the existence of a dual certificate
div.formula: q := - 1 λ ∇ F ( θλ ) ∈ ∂R( θλ ),
div.p: As the previous abstract theorem shows, to ensure model consistency of θλ , it is sufficient to ensure estimator proximity to θ ⋆ and dual certificate stability of q to a suitable element q ⋆ ∈ ∂R(θ ⋆ ). Estimator proximity is given in Theorem 1 whose assumptions as verified for the iUOT setting in Theorem 3. We now discuss convergence of the dual certificate.

The question is the limit of q as μ converges to µ ⋆ and Ω converges to Ω. We define a canonical dual certificate, which will turn out to be the limit of q: Definition 3 (Minimal Norm Certificate). Assume F is twice differentiable and that H ⋆ := ∇ 2 F (θ ⋆ ) is invertible. The minimal norm certificate is defined as
div.formula: q ⋆ := argmin z∈∂R(θ ⋆ ) ⟨z, (H ⋆ ) -1 z⟩.
div.p: It is said to be nondegenerate if q ⋆ ∈ ri(∂R(θ ⋆ )). Indeed, the following proposition shows that q ⋆ is the limit certificate at the population level, under smoothness assumptions on F . Proposition 6. Let F : R n → R be a strictly convex and differentiable function. Let R be a convex, Lipschitz continuous function. For λ ⩾ 0, let
div.formula: θ λ ∈ argmin θ F (θ) + λR(θ).
div.p: Suppose that F is twice differentiable at θ ⋆ , where θ ⋆ = θ0 , and that ∇ 2 F (θ ⋆ ) is invertible. Then, the limit lim λ→0 -∇F (θ λ )/λ exists and is equal to
div.formula: q ⋆ = argmin q∈∂R(θ ⋆ ) q, ∇ 2 F (θ ⋆ ) -1 q . (23

div.head: Model Consistency for iUOT with smooth divergences
div.p: In the following, let
div.formula: F(θ) = L iUOT (Ψθ; π ⋆ , α ⋆ , β ⋆ ) and F n (θ) = L iUOT (Ψθ; π n , α n , β n ). Let θ λ ∈ arg min θ F(θ) + λR(θ) and θλ n ∈ arg min θ F n (θ) + λR(θ).
div.p: Theorem 4. Assume that Assumptions 1, 2, 3 and 4 hold. Assume further that ϕ * 1 and ϕ * 2 are C ∞ . Let R be a mirror stratifiable regularizer. Let q ⋆ be the minimal norm certificate and assume that q ⋆ ∈ ri(∂R(θ ⋆ )). Let θλn n be the iUOT regularized solution with n samples and regularization parameter λ n . Provided that λ n n/ log(n) → ∞ and λ n → 0, for all n sufficiently large, with probability 1, M θ * = M θ n,λn .

Moreover, ∇F n ( θλ ∞ ) + λp = ∇F n ( θλ ∞ ) -∇F( θλ ∞ ) = Ψ * π n -Ψ * π ⋆ A 1 + Ψ * π -Ψ * πn A 2 , where πn = argmin π∈M + (X ×Y) ⟨Ψθ λ ∞ , π n ⟩ + ηKL(π|α n ⊗ β n ) + D φ 1 (π 1 |α n ) + D φ 2 (π 2 |β n ). and π = argmin π∈M + (X ×Y) ⟨Ψθ λ ∞ , π ⋆ ⟩ + ηKL(π|α ⋆ ⊗ β ⋆ ) + D φ 1 (π 1 |α ⋆ ) + D φ 2 (π 2 |β ⋆ ).
div.p: We have ∥A 1 ∥ ⩽ t/ √ n with probability at least 1-e -t 2 by Hoeffing's inequality. Moreover, A 2 is corresponds exactly to the error of the forward UOT problem with cost Ψθ λ ∞ (note that this is not a random object), so directly applying Theorem 2 implies that ∥A 2 ∥ ⩽ m(α)m(β)(log(n)+t) n with probability at least 1 -e -t .
So, ∇F n ( θλ

Then, since n P(E c n ) ⩽ n 1 n 2 < ∞, by the Borel Cantelli theorem, with probability 1, E n does not hold for at most finitely many n. It follows that with probability 1, q λn n -q 0 → 0 and θ λn n -θ ⋆ → 0 with probability 1.

div.head: Numerics
div.p: The iUOT objective function is differentiable, so for the regularized iUOT optimization problem, one could apply projected gradient descent, or combine projected gradient descent steps with the Sinkhorn algorithm. An alternative, as we explain in this section, is to exploit the semidual formulation of UOT. To handle the ℓ 1 and nuclear norm regularizations, we advocate the use of their quadratic variational form to obtain a problem that is amenable to quasi-Newton method such as L-BFGS.

Quadratic variational forms Although the ℓ 1 and nuclear norm regularizers can be handled using proximal operators, these regularizers also have quadratic variational forms which are amenable to smooth optimization tecniques. Namely,
div.formula: ∥θ∥ 1 = 1 2 min θ=uv ∥u∥ 2 + ∥v∥ 2 and ∥θ∥ * = 1 2 min θ=uv ∥u∥ 2 F + ∥v∥ 2 F ,
div.p: where as a slight abuse of notation, in the ℓ 1 case over θ ∈ R p , uv = (u i v i ) p i=1 is the Hadamard product, and in the case of nuclear norm on θ ∈ R p 1 ×p 2 , the minimum is over all matrices u ∈ R p 1 ×p 1 and v ∈ R p 2 ×p 2 and uv is the product of two matrices. In these cases, we can replace the regularizer R(θ) with the differentiable term W(u, v). We are therefore led to solve min

Often, for fixed g, u, v, the minimization H(g, c) := min f K(f, g, c) has a closed form expression, e.g. in the case where ϕ 1 is the indicator function or KL []. The so called semidual formulation is then min
div.formula: g,u,v H(g, uv) + λW(u, v)
div.p: and can be handled using quasi-Newton solvers such as L-BFGS. We refer to [2] for the case of iOT.
The case of iJKO In the case of iJKO, recalling the Kantorovich formulation (Kanto-iJKO), the infimum over g has a closed form solution
div.formula: g = - η τ log exp (τ (f (x) -c V (x, •)/η) dα k+1 (x) .
div.p: So, we also have
div.formula: L iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) = min f S(V, f ) with S(V, f ) := ⟨V + s exp(- f s ), α k+1 ⟩ + η τ log exp (τ (f (x) -c V (x, •)/η) dα k+1 (x) dα k . (Semidual-iJKO) leading to L iJKO (V ; α k , α k+1 ) = min f S(V, f ). In practice, given α k+1 = 1 M M i=1 δ y i and α k = 1 N N i=1 δ x i ,
div.p: and a linear parametrization of the cost V (x) = Ψ(θ)(x) for some θ ∈ R s and with regularization R, we solve inf
div.formula: θ∈R s ,f ∈R M λR(θ) + 1 M M i=1 Φ(θ)(y i ) + s M M i=1 exp(- f i s ) + 1 N N j=1 η τ log 1 M M i=1 exp η -1 τ f i -η -1 τ Φ(θ)(y i ) -η -1 ∥y i -x j ∥ 2 .
div.p: In the case of ℓ 1 or nuclear norm regularization, we can make use of the Hadamard product formulation to solve the following smooth optimization problem inf
div.formula: X,Y ∈R s ,f ∈R M λ 2 ∥(X, Y )∥ 2 + 1 M M i=1 Φ(X ⊙ Y )(y i ) + s M M i=1 exp(- f i s ) + 1 N N j=1 η τ log 1 M M i=1 exp 1 η τ f i -τ Ψ(X ⊙ Y )(y i ) -∥y i -x j ∥ 2 .

div.head: Learning quadratic potentials in iJKO on Gaussians
div.p: To investigate numerically the model consistency behaviour of iJKO, we consider a simple setting where the distributions α k are Gaussian, and the potential functions of interest are quadratics V θ (x) = x ⊤ θx.
Setup Let θ ∈ R d×d , and consider
div.formula: d dt x t = -∇V ⋆ (x t )(27)
div.p: where V ⋆ (x) = x ⊤ θ ⋆ x, and let x 0 ∼ N (m ⋆ , Σ ⋆ ). We denote the distribution of x t by α t . Then, α t remains a Gaussian with α t = N (m t , Σ t ) where
div.formula: m t = e -2tθ ⋆ m 0 , Σ t = e -2tθ ⋆ Σ 0 e -2tθ ⋆ .
div.p: For k = 0, 1, . . . , T , we observe n iid samples drawn from ρ τ k . We will consider different settings and numerically demonstrate how non-degeneracy of a minimal norm certificate prescribes the stability properties of iJKO.

div.head: Certificate formulas in the Gaussian setting
div.p: Since there is in general no closed form expressions for our loss F , directly studying the dual certificates is challenging. To this end, we recall that the sharpened F-Y loss of iJKO corresponds to a quadratic function, and letting the sharpening parameter r → ∞, in the Gaussian setting where α i = N (m i , Σ i ) and V θ (x) = x ⊤ θx, loss given α k+1 and α k corresponds to
div.formula: J F Y (θ) := T -1 k=0 F iJKO (θ; α k+1 , α k ) = T -1 k=0 Σ 1/2 α k+1 θ + τ -1 ∇ 1 B 2 η (Σ α k+1 , Σ α k ) Σ 1/2 α k+1 2 F + 2 Σ 1/2 α k+1 θm α k+1 + τ -1 (m α k+1 -m α k )2 2
div.p: where B 2 η is the squared Bures metric, and
div.formula: ∇ 1 B 2 η (A, B) = Id-B 1 2 (B 1 2 AB 1 2 + η 4 Id) 1 2 + η 2 Id -1 B 1 2 . The Hessian of J F Y is the matrix ∇ 2 J F Y (θ) = H F Y := T -1 k=0 2Σ α k+1 ⊗ Σ α k+1 + 4Σ α k+1 ⊗ (m α k+1 ⊗ m α k+1 ),
div.p: Precertificate Given the Hessian matrix H, a useful proxy for the minimal norm certificate q ⋆ is q := HP T (P T HP T ) -1 S T (θ ⋆ ) = argmin z ⟨z, (H ⋆ ) -1 z⟩ ; P T z = S T (θ ⋆ ) , where • In the case of ℓ 1 , letting I = Supp(θ), P T v = v I , and S T (θ) = sign(v I ). The precertificate q is nondegenerate when ∥P ⊥ T q∥ ∞ < 1. • In the case of nuclear norm, letting U SV ⊤ = θ be the singular value decomposition of θ, S T (θ) = U V ⊤ and
div.formula: P T = Id ⊗ U U ⊤ + V V ⊤ ⊗ I -V V ⊤ ⊗ U U ⊤ .
div.p: The precertificate q is nondegenerate when ∥P ⊥ T q∥ 2 < 1. If q is nondegenerate, then since the constraints of q ⋆ in Definition 3 do not saturate (for ℓ 1 , the constraints are ∥q ⋆ ∥ ∞ ⩽ 1 and for nuclear norm, the constraints are ∥q ⋆ ∥ 2 ⩽ 1), one must have q = q ⋆ . Remark 5. Some situations in which we will be nondegenerate in the ℓ 1 setting:
• If m α 0 = 0 and Σ α 0 is diagonal, then H is a diagonal matrix and the certificate is always nondegenerate.
• If m α 0 ̸ = 0 and Σ α 0 = σId, then as σ → ∞, the contribution of m will diminish and the certificate becomes nondegenerate as σ increases.

div.head: Numerical experiments with Gaussians for linear iJKO
div.p: Having described in the setting and computation of dual certificates, we now consider different settings where we compute the corresponding q and check the model consistency performance under sampled data.

Low rank setting We consider the case where θ ⋆ is a low rank matrix. For some small δ > 0 (we choose δ = 1e -2 here), let θ ⋆ = δId + r i=1 u i u ⊤ i be a rank r matrix and assume that Σ ⋆ = r i=1 u ϕ i (u ϕ i ) ⊤ , where u ϕ i = cos(ϕ)u i +sin(ϕ)v i is also low rank, where {u i } i ∪{v i } i is a set of orthonormal vectors. We will vary ϕ and fix the mean as m ⋆ = 0. Figure 1 shows how the trajectories change as ϕ goes from 0 to π/2. When ϕ = 0, the eigenvectors of Σ t is perfectly aligned with θ ⋆ for all t. When ϕ = π/2, the eigenvectors of Σ t are orthogonal to those of θ ⋆ for all t. The nondegeneracy of the certificates are shown in Figure 2 -when ϕ becomes close to π/2, the samples are aligned in an orthogonal direction to θ ⋆ and the certificate becomes degenerate. On the other hand, the certificate becomes nondegenerate when ϕ is close to 0. In Figure 3, we test the recovery performance where we take T = 2 time points, and consider Σ ⋆ for different values of ϕ in the case of d = 2. The true matrix θ ⋆ is a rank 1 matrix with eignevalues 1 and 0. We fix s = 1, ε = 10 -4 and for each value of N , we grid search over λ (in the range [10 -10 , 10 -2 ]) and plot the error ∥ Ũ Ũ ⊤ -U ⋆ (U ⋆ ) ⊤ ∥ 2 where U ⋆ is the true eigenvectors of θ ⋆ , and U consists of the reconstructed eigenvectors, where an eigenvector is included if its eigenvalue is at least 10 -2 is absolute value.

The sparse setting We consider the recovery of θ ⋆ representing the adjacency matrix of a circular graph (see Figure 4). Since θ ⋆ is sparse non-negative, we consider its stabilty properties with ℓ 1 -regularization restricted to non-negative θ, and a certificate z is nondegenerate with respect to the true support I of θ ⋆ if z max := max i̸ ∈I z i < 1. In Figure 5, we present the certificates for m ⋆ = c1 and Σ ⋆ = w 2 Id for different values of c and w. Consider the case of m = 1. When w is small, the certificate becomes degenerate. Figure 6 provides a visualization of how the populations evolve. From the nondegeneracy graphs of Figure 5, we expect that in the case of m ⋆ = 1, with T = 2, the support of θ ⋆ can be stably recovered when w = 0.2 and cannot be be stably recovered when w = 1. On the other hand, if we increase T to T = 6, ϕ = 0 ϕ = 0.1π ϕ = 0.3π ϕ = 0.4π Figure 3: Here, both θ ⋆ = δId + u ⋆ (u ⋆ ) ⊤ and Σ ⋆ = vv ⊤ is a rank 1 matrix, and we vary ϕ between u ⋆ and v. The x-axis shows in log-scale the parameter λ and the y-axis shows ∥ Ũλ Ũ ⊤ λ -u ⋆ (u ⋆ ) ⊤ ∥ 2 where Ũλ are the reconstructed eigenvectors. Observe that the eigenvectors are better recovered for small ϕ, which corroborates the fact the the certificate is nondegenerate when ϕ is small. then θ ⋆ can be stably recovered with w = 0.2. We numerically verify this in Figure 7, where one can observe this behaviour as the number of samples N increases.

div.head: Comparisons to the lightspeed loss
div.p: In the case where V (x) = x ⊤ θx and α k ∼ N (m k , Σ k ) are Gaussians, the lightspeed loss is
and the Hessian of J LS is
div.formula: H LS := T -1 k=0 2Id ⊗ Σ α k+1 + 2Id ⊗ (m α k+1 ⊗ m α k+1 ).
div.p: NB: the hessian of H LS is always block diagonal, whereas H F Y could be a full matrix.

In figure 8, we investigate the difference between the lightspeed precertificate (LS) and the Fenchel-Young precertificate (FY) for sparse recovery (i.e. ℓ 1 regularization), here, we set m ⋆ = 0.11 and Σ ⋆ = w 2 Id and vary w, and θ ⋆ ∈ R 4×4 is the adjacency matrix of a circular graph as considered in the previous section. There is little difference between the two for T = 2, but as T increases, FY is nondegenerate for a greater range of parameters w. Observe that at w = 0.4, FY is degenerate for T = 2 bu is nondegenerate for T = 4. On the other hand, LS is degenerate at w = 0.4 for T = 2 and T = 4. This difference of behaviour in the certificates is reflected in the sparse recovery performance as demonstrated in Figure 9.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: We introduced a general methodology for solving inverse problems over the space of probability measures using sharpened Fenchel-Young losses. While our analysis centered on two instances -iUOT and iJKO -rooted in optimal transport theory, the framework itself is broadly applicable. The key ingredients of our approach, namely convexity, differentiability, and sample-dependent stability, make it a flexible tool for a wider class of inverse problems involving distributions. In particular, this opens up promising directions for future work in generative modeling, where learning mechanisms from sampled data is central.

Since F is convex and θ is an isolated fixed point, θ is a unique minimizer of F . The result follows by plugging in g( θ) = θ to the error bound above.

div.head: C Basic Results on Unbalanced OT
div.p: This section, gathers the fundamental properties of unbalanced optimal transport that are recurrently used throughout this work (see [52] for a reference on most of these results). div.head: D Proofs for forward UOT problem D.1 Compact anchoring and continuity of Kantorovich potentials
div.p: For balanced OT, the function K = K α,β is translation invariant in the sense that
div.formula: ∀λ ∈ R, K(f + λ, g -λ) = K(f, g).
div.p: For the general UOT problem, one can associate optimization of K to optimization of a translation invariant function T . Note that the set P 0 given in the Proposition below is also compact in C(X ) × C(Y).

Proposition 11 (The quotient dual potentials vary continuously with the input measures).

Let φ 1 and φ 2 be lsc and convex. Under Assumption 1, if α n ⇀ α and β n ⇀ β, then (f n , g n ) ∈ P α n ,β n 0 minimizing the associated translation invariant operator T α n ,β n n converge uniformly to Kantorovich potentials of (f, g) ∈ P α,β 0 minimizing T α,β . It follows that lim n→∞ ⟨π n -π, h⟩ = 0 for all test function h ∈ C(X × Y), where π n = exp(f n ⊕ g n -c)α n ⊗ β n . Proof. By strong convexity of T α n ,β n , (f n , g n ) are uniquely defined. We know that m α n → m α and m β n → m β . So, by Proposition 10, we can assume that ∥f n ∥ ∞ , ∥g n ∥ ∞ are uniformly bounded. By Arzela Ascoli, f n k → f and g n k → g uniformly -this is because the optimality condition would be preserved in the limit. The pairs (f, g) are the unique minimizers to T with (α, β). So, the entire sequence converges uniformly to (f, g).

div.head: D.2 Restriction to compact sets
div.p: The following proposition shows that under assumption 2, one can further restrict the optimization over (f, g) to compact sets. One consequence of this is, as shown in the next section, when one of the divergences is locally strongly convex, K is locally strongly convex and since optimization over K can be restricted to a bounded set, we can make use of the PL inequality to prove sample complexity rates.

Our proof is based on the Fenchel-Young inequality. While a similar result has been established in previous works [53], different proof strategies were employed for different divergence terms in [53]: there was one proof ([53, Lemma 5]) for the situation where φ * is strictly convex and that there exist s n ∈ ∂φ * (x n ) such that s n → 0 or s n → ∞, and the cases of TV and range divergences were handled separately. In contrast, our assumption in iii) below encompasses all the cases described in [53].
Proposition 12. [Restriction to compact sets] Under Assumptions 1 and 2, we can further restrict the optimization in T α,β to λ ∈ [-R, R] for some R > 0, i.e.
T α,β (f, g) = min λ∈[-R,R] K α,β (f + λ, g -λ).
Consequently, the minimizers f, g of K α,β satisfy ∥f ∥ ∞ ⩽ M and ∥g∥ ∞ ⩽ M for some constant M > 0.
The constants M, R depend only on m α , m β , diam(X ), diam(Y) and c.

Proof. div.head: A Quantifying approximate optimality conditions
div.p: The proof of the theorem 1 hinges on the following technical lemma. On a high level, the lemma quantifies how, for a locally Lipschitz smooth and locally strongly convex function F , an approximate first order condition at a point θ ⋆ relates to the proximity of θ ⋆ to the global minimum θ ∈ argmin θ F (θ). In our main stability theorem, F represents an empirical loss function, and if θ ⋆ is the estimate corresponding to the population loss, assuming that ∇ F (θ ⋆ ) is small, we will use this result to show that the minimizer to F remains close to θ ⋆ . Lemma 2. Let Θ be a Hilbert space with norm ∥•∥. Let F : Θ → R be a differentiable convex function. Let θ ⋆ ∈ Θ. Assume that F is α-strongly convex and ∇ F is L-Lipschitz on the ball B(0; K) of radius K > ∥θ ⋆ ∥. Let R : Θ → [0, ∞] be a convex proper lsc function.
The following hold.
i) If ∇ F (θ ⋆ ) ⩽ ε, where ε < α(K -∥θ ⋆ ∥), then F has a unique minimizer θ in the ball B(θ ⋆ ; r) where r ∈ [ ε α , K -∥θ∥]. In particular, θ -θ ⋆ ⩽ α -1 ε.
Since T is nonexpansive, ∥g(θ) -θ∥ ⩽ τ ε, and we can now proceed as in the proof of (i).
